items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: When you push a wall, what does Newton's third law say about the wall?
items.correct: The wall exerts an equal and opposite force on you at the same instant.
items.misconception: The wall only pushes back if your force is strong enough to make it react.
items.random_wrong: The wall pushes back with a force proportional to how stiff it is, not equal to your force.
items.why: Students think action-reaction is causal and sequential — push first, then the wall 'responds' — instead of being two aspects of one interaction.

items.q: In what direction does a magnetic field exert a force on a current-carrying wire?
items.correct: Perpendicular to both the current direction and the magnetic field direction (Fleming's left hand rule).
items.misconception: The force is in the same direction as the magnetic field.
items.random_wrong: The force is in the same direction as the current in the wire.
items.why: Students expect forces to act 'along' visible directions; the perpendicular result is counterintuitive without a vector cross product mental model.

items.q: Which law explains why a rocket accelerates as it expels exhaust?
items.why: Correct answer: 3rd Law. “1st Law” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Net force on an object moving at constant velocity is:
items.why: Correct answer: zero. “mg” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Net force 50 N on a 10 kg mass. Acceleration in m/s²?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
